network.overall_strategy: The network constructs L-valine through multiple convergent prebiotic routes anchored in the Patel et al. (2015) cyanosulfidic protometabolism framework. The primary pathway proceeds from C3 sugar derivatives (dihydroxyacetone, hydroxyacetone) through reductive deoxygenation to acetone (a central hub), followed by cyanohydrin formation, thioamide conversion, Cu/H2S-mediated reductive rearrangement with chain extension, Strecker-type amination, and nitrile hydrolysis. Upstream hydrothermal chemistry provides C1 feedstocks (formate, formaldehyde) via CO2 reduction on iron-sulfur minerals, which feed into surface formose chemistry to generate the C3 sugar precursors. A parallel Strecker route via isobutyraldehyde (from Fischer-Tropsch-type synthesis) and a hydrothermal reductive amination route via alpha-ketoisovalerate provide alternative environmental pathways to valine. The network features five hub molecules (formaldehyde, acetone, HCN, 2-hydroxy-3-methylbutanenitrile, and 2-amino-3-methylbutanenitrile) creating dense interconnection and redundancy across hydrothermal, surface, and biochemical environments.

reactions.name: CO2 reduction to formate on greigite

conditions.other: Alkaline hydrothermal vent conditions, pH 9-11, H2-rich fluids from serpentinization

reactions.mechanism: CO2 is adsorbed onto the Fe3S4 greigite surface, which is structurally analogous to biological [Fe4S4] clusters. Molecular hydrogen dissociatively adsorbs on the mineral surface, providing hydride equivalents that reduce CO2 via a two-electron transfer to formate (HCOO-). The reaction is thermodynamically favorable under the highly reducing, alkaline vent conditions.
reactions.reasoning: Greigite-catalyzed CO2 reduction to formate is a well-established hydrothermal reaction analogous to the Wood-Ljungdahl pathway. Demonstrated by Preiner et al. (2020, Nature Ecology & Evolution) and Herschy et al. (2014), showing that native iron-sulfur minerals catalyze CO2 fixation under simulated alkaline vent conditions. This provides the C1 feedstock for downstream formaldehyde production.

reactions.name: Formate reduction to formaldehyde on magnetite

conditions.other: Subseafloor hydrothermal conditions, Fe0 as strong reductant

reactions.mechanism: Formate is further reduced on Fe3O4/Fe0 surfaces through a two-electron reduction that removes one oxygen, yielding formaldehyde (H2CO). Elemental iron provides the driving force for reduction, being oxidized to Fe2+ in the process. Magnetite serves as the catalytic surface facilitating hydrogen transfer.
reactions.reasoning: Reduction of formate to formaldehyde under hydrothermal conditions is supported by work on serpentinization-driven organic synthesis (McCollom & Seewald, 2007, EPSL; Seewald et al., 2006). Formaldehyde is a critical C1 building block for the formose reaction that produces the C3 sugar precursors needed for the downstream cyanosulfidic valine pathway. This step connects hydrothermal CO2 fixation to surface sugar chemistry.

reactions.name: Formaldehyde self-condensation to glycolaldehyde (formose initiation)

conditions.other: Alkaline conditions (pH 10-11), Ca(OH)2 present, evaporitic pool setting

reactions.mechanism: Two molecules of formaldehyde undergo an aldol-type condensation catalyzed by Ca(OH)2 and clay mineral surfaces. The Cannizzaro reaction provides initial glycolaldehyde through disproportionation, and subsequent aldol addition of formaldehyde to glycolaldehyde creates C3 sugars. Borate minerals stabilize the sugar products against degradation by forming borate esters.
reactions.reasoning: The formose reaction (Butlerow, 1861) is the classical prebiotic route to sugars from formaldehyde. Ricardo et al. (2004, Science) demonstrated that borate minerals stabilize sugars (especially ribose) against degradation, enabling productive formose chemistry. Montmorillonite clay catalyzes the condensation and concentrates reactants. This produces the glycolaldehyde intermediate needed for C3 sugar synthesis.

reactions.name: Glycolaldehyde + formaldehyde to dihydroxyacetone (formose chain extension)

conditions.other: Alkaline formose conditions, borate stabilization, wet-dry cycling in evaporitic pools

reactions.mechanism: Glycolaldehyde undergoes aldol condensation with formaldehyde to form glyceraldehyde (C3 aldose), which then isomerizes via a 1,2-hydride shift (Lobry de Bruyn-van Ekenstein rearrangement) to dihydroxyacetone (C3 ketose). Borate complexation stabilizes the triose products and shifts the equilibrium toward the ketose form.
reactions.reasoning: Formose-type aldol chain extension from glycolaldehyde to C3 sugars is well documented (Breslow, 1959; Orgel, 2000). Borate stabilization of the triose is demonstrated by Ricardo et al. (2004, Science). Dihydroxyacetone is the specific C3 sugar feedstock required for the Patel et al. (2015) cyanosulfidic valine pathway.

reactions.name: Glycolaldehyde to hydroxyacetone via formose methylation

conditions.other: Formose reaction conditions, alternative branching pathway under slightly different pH/concentration

reactions.mechanism: Under formose conditions, glycolaldehyde undergoes retro-aldol/aldol rearrangement with formaldehyde, producing hydroxyacetone (acetol, CH3COCH2OH) as an alternative C3 product alongside dihydroxyacetone. The methyl group of hydroxyacetone arises from dehydration-rearrangement of an intermediate aldol adduct.
reactions.reasoning: Hydroxyacetone is a known product of the formose reaction alongside dihydroxyacetone and glyceraldehyde (Mizuno & Weiss, 1974). Patel et al. (2015, Nature Chemistry) explicitly use hydroxyacetone as an alternative starting sugar for the cyanosulfidic valine pathway. This provides a second upstream entry point to the acetone hub.

reactions.name: Dihydroxyacetone reductive deoxygenation to acetone

conditions.other: Cyanosulfidic conditions: Cu0 as single-electron reductant, H2S as co-reductant, surface evaporitic pool

reactions.mechanism: Copper acts as a single-electron reductant in concert with hydrogen sulfide, stripping both hydroxymethyl groups from dihydroxyacetone and reducing the C3 backbone to acetone. Cu0 is oxidized to Cu+ or Cu2+ during the transformation, while H2S is oxidized to disulfane (H2S2).
reactions.reasoning: Patel, Percivalle, Ritson, Duffy, and Sutherland (2015, Nature Chemistry, DOI: 10.1038/nchem.2202) demonstrated this reductive deoxygenation as Step 1 of the cyanosulfidic valine pathway. Copper and H2S are essential reagents in their unified cyanosulfidic protometabolism framework that converts sugar feedstocks into ketone intermediates suitable for downstream nitrile chemistry.

reactions.name: Hydroxyacetone reductive deoxygenation to acetone

conditions.other: Cyanosulfidic conditions: Cu0 as single-electron reductant, H2S as co-reductant

reactions.mechanism: Copper/H2S-mediated reductive removal of the hydroxyl group from hydroxyacetone (acetol), yielding acetone. The mechanism is analogous to rxn_006 but requires removal of only one hydroxymethyl group.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. (2015, Nature Chemistry, DOI: 10.1038/nchem.2202) demonstrated this as an alternative upstream entry to the acetone hub from a different sugar derivative. This provides pathway redundancy: both DHA and hydroxyacetone converge on the same acetone intermediate under identical cyanosulfidic conditions.

reactions.name: Cyanohydrin formation: acetone + HCN

conditions.other: Cyanosulfidic pool conditions, mildly basic pH favoring CN- nucleophile

reactions.mechanism: Nucleophilic addition of cyanide ion (CN-) to the electrophilic carbonyl carbon of acetone. The cyanide attacks the sp2 carbon, and subsequent protonation of the resulting alkoxide yields the tertiary cyanohydrin (acetone cyanohydrin). The symmetrical dimethyl substitution of acetone produces a single cyanohydrin product.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. (2015, Nature Chemistry, DOI: 10.1038/nchem.2202) demonstrated this cyanohydrin formation as Step 2 of the cyanosulfidic valine pathway. This is a classic Kiliani-type reaction (ketone variant) that introduces the C-CN bond that will ultimately become the carboxylic acid group of valine.

reactions.name: Thioamide formation: cyanohydrin + H2S

conditions.other: Cyanosulfidic conditions, H2S acts as both reagent and part of the redox environment

reactions.mechanism: H2S adds across the nitrile (C≡N) triple bond of the cyanohydrin. The sulfur nucleophile attacks the electrophilic nitrile carbon, and proton transfer completes the conversion to a thioamide (C(=S)NH2). This activates the nitrile for subsequent reductive chemistry by replacing the strong C≡N triple bond with a more reactive thioamide functionality.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. (2015, Nature Chemistry, DOI: 10.1038/nchem.2202) demonstrated this as Step 3 of the cyanosulfidic valine pathway. Thioamide formation is a key step in the cyanosulfidic framework: conversion of nitriles to thioamides via H2S activates the molecule for the critical Cu-mediated reductive rearrangement that builds the branched valine carbon skeleton.

reactions.name: Reductive rearrangement with chain extension (Cu/H2S-mediated)

conditions.other: Cyanosulfidic conditions, Cu0 as single-electron reductant, H2S as co-reductant and sulfur source

reactions.mechanism: This is a complex, multi-step cyanosulfidic transformation. The thioamide undergoes reductive rearrangement mediated by copper and hydrogen sulfide. The thioamide nitrogen is lost, and a new C–C bond is formed by incorporation of a second equivalent of HCN. Elemental copper acts as a single-electron reductant, while H2S provides reducing equivalents and sulfur acts as a leaving group (forming disulfane, H2S2). The net result is chain extension: the C4 thioamide is converted to a C5 alpha-hydroxynitrile with an isopropyl branch, establishing the valine carbon skeleton.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. (2015, Nature Chemistry, DOI: 10.1038/nchem.2202) demonstrated this as Step 4 of the cyanosulfidic valine pathway — the critical C–C bond-forming step that builds the branched-chain architecture unique to valine. This is the most mechanistically complex step in the network and represents the key innovation of the cyanosulfidic approach: using Cu/H2S redox chemistry to achieve C-C bond formation through thioamide intermediates.

reactions.name: Strecker-type amination of alpha-hydroxynitrile

conditions.other: Cyanosulfidic pool, NH3 available from volcanic outgassing or HCN hydrolysis

reactions.mechanism: Nucleophilic substitution of the alpha-hydroxyl group by ammonia. The OH is displaced by NH3, converting the hydroxynitrile to an aminonitrile. This is a Strecker-type amination step — the replacement of OH by NH2 at the alpha-carbon adjacent to the nitrile — producing the direct aminonitrile precursor of valine.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. (2015, Nature Chemistry, DOI: 10.1038/nchem.2202) demonstrated this as Step 5 of the cyanosulfidic valine pathway. The Strecker-type amination converts the alpha-hydroxynitrile to the aminonitrile, setting up the final hydrolysis to the amino acid. This reaction is well-precedented in prebiotic chemistry (Strecker, 1854; Miller, 1957).

reactions.name: Nitrile hydrolysis of 2-amino-3-methylbutanenitrile to L-valine

conditions.other: Hydrolysis in aqueous pool, possibly aided by wet-dry cycling and mild heating

reactions.mechanism: Hydrolysis of the nitrile group of the aminonitrile to the carboxylic acid. Water attacks the electrophilic nitrile carbon, forming an amide intermediate (2-amino-3-methylbutanamide), which further hydrolyzes to the free carboxylic acid with release of ammonia. This is the final Strecker-type hydrolysis step that converts the aminonitrile to L-valine.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. (2015, Nature Chemistry, DOI: 10.1038/nchem.2202) demonstrated this as the final step (Step 6) of the cyanosulfidic valine pathway. Nitrile hydrolysis to carboxylic acids is thermodynamically favorable and kinetically accessible under mild aqueous conditions, especially with clay mineral catalysis (Ferris et al., 1996).

reactions.name: Nitrile hydrolysis of 2-amino-4-methylpentanenitrile to L-valine (Pathway 2)

conditions.other: Hydrolysis with concomitant chain shortening, aqueous pool conditions

reactions.mechanism: Hydrolysis of the nitrile group of the C6 aminonitrile with concomitant loss of one carbon (chain shortening), converting the C6 aminonitrile to the C5 amino acid valine. The chain shortening may occur through a retro-aldol or decarboxylative mechanism under the cyanosulfidic conditions, releasing one CH2 unit as formaldehyde or CO2.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. (2015, Nature Chemistry, DOI: 10.1038/nchem.2202) described this as an alternative route to valine from the C6 aminonitrile produced through the hydroxyacetone branch of the cyanosulfidic network. The chain-shortening mechanism remains an open question in the literature.

reactions.name: Fischer-Tropsch-type synthesis of isobutyraldehyde

conditions.other: Fischer-Tropsch-type synthesis at black smoker or subseafloor hydrothermal conditions

reactions.mechanism: Carbon monoxide and hydrogen gas undergo Fischer-Tropsch-type (FTT) synthesis on magnetite surfaces, producing a mixture of hydrocarbons, alcohols, and aldehydes. Isobutyraldehyde (2-methylpropanal) is a branched C4 aldehyde product formed through chain growth and branching on the mineral surface. CO adsorbs dissociatively, and sequential hydrogenation/chain growth steps build the branched carbon skeleton.
reactions.reasoning: Fischer-Tropsch-type synthesis of aldehydes and hydrocarbons on magnetite under hydrothermal conditions was demonstrated by McCollom et al. (1999, EPSL) and Rushdi & Simoneit (2001). Branched aldehydes including isobutyraldehyde are plausible products of FTT synthesis, providing a hydrothermal route to the Strecker aldehyde precursor of valine.

reactions.name: Classical Strecker synthesis of valine aminonitrile from isobutyraldehyde

conditions.other: Strecker conditions: aldehyde + NH3 + HCN in aqueous solution

reactions.mechanism: Classical Strecker synthesis: (1) Ammonia attacks the aldehyde carbonyl of isobutyraldehyde to form a hemiaminal, which dehydrates to an imine (Schiff base). (2) Cyanide ion (CN-) then adds to the electrophilic imine carbon in a nucleophilic addition, yielding 2-amino-3-methylbutanenitrile — the same aminonitrile produced by the cyanosulfidic pathway. This provides a direct, well-characterized route from a branched aldehyde to the valine aminonitrile.
reactions.reasoning: The Strecker synthesis is one of the oldest and most established prebiotic amino acid syntheses (Strecker, 1854; Miller, 1953). Miller's spark discharge experiments demonstrated that branched-chain amino acids including valine are produced via Strecker chemistry from aldehydes generated in the reducing atmosphere. The use of isobutyraldehyde as the specific aldehyde precursor for valine Strecker synthesis is described in standard prebiotic chemistry references (Miller & Orgel, 1974).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal reductive amination of alpha-ketoisovalerate to valine

conditions.other: Alkaline hydrothermal vent conditions, FeS/FeS2 surface catalysis, NH3 from vent fluids

reactions.mechanism: Reductive amination of the alpha-keto acid on iron-sulfur mineral surfaces. The carbonyl oxygen of alpha-ketoisovalerate is attacked by ammonia to form an imine (Schiff base), which is then reduced by H2 or FeS-derived electrons to yield L-valine. The iron-sulfur mineral surface provides both the catalytic site and the reducing equivalents for imine reduction.
reactions.reasoning: Reductive amination of alpha-keto acids on iron-sulfur mineral surfaces is supported by the work of Huber & Wächtershäuser (2003, Tetrahedron Letters) and Barge et al. (2019, PNAS), who demonstrated amino acid synthesis from keto acids and ammonia under hydrothermal conditions. This provides a direct hydrothermal route to valine that bypasses the surface cyanosulfidic pathway entirely, representing an alternative environmental context for valine formation.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal synthesis of alpha-ketoisovalerate from pyruvate and formaldehyde

conditions.other: Multi-step process: CO2 reduction to pyruvate on greigite, then aldol with formaldehyde and reduction/dehydration

reactions.mechanism: Alpha-ketoisovalerate is synthesized through a multi-step process on iron-sulfur mineral surfaces: (1) CO2 is reduced to pyruvate on greigite/magnetite surfaces (analogous to reductive TCA cycle intermediates). (2) Pyruvate undergoes aldol condensation with formaldehyde and a second carbon unit, followed by reduction and dehydration/rearrangement to yield the branched C5 alpha-keto acid. The mineral surfaces template the carbon-carbon bond-forming steps.
reactions.reasoning: This reaction is partially speculative but grounded in demonstrated hydrothermal CO2 fixation to pyruvate (Cody et al., 2000, Science; Varma et al., 2018, Nature Ecology & Evolution) and the general principle that alpha-keto acids are central hubs of prebiotic metabolism. The specific construction of the branched C5 skeleton from C1/C3 units on mineral surfaces extends the demonstrated chemistry of CO2 fixation on iron-sulfur minerals. This step is necessary to connect the hydrothermal reductive amination route (rxn_016) to the upstream feedstock network.

pathways.name: Full Hydrothermal-to-Surface Cyanosulfidic Route via DHA
pathways.description: Complete pathway from hydrothermal CO2 fixation through formose sugar synthesis to cyanosulfidic valine production via dihydroxyacetone. Represents the most complete environmental interconnection from deep-sea vent CO2 to surface amino acid.

pathways.name: Full Hydrothermal-to-Surface Cyanosulfidic Route via Hydroxyacetone
pathways.description: Complete pathway from hydrothermal CO2 fixation through formose chemistry to cyanosulfidic valine production via hydroxyacetone. Parallels P1 but uses the alternative C3 sugar feedstock.

pathways.name: Surface-only Cyanosulfidic Route from Dihydroxyacetone
pathways.description: The primary Patel et al. (2015) cyanosulfidic pathway starting from dihydroxyacetone assumed available in surface pools. Six-step sequence: reductive deoxygenation, cyanohydrin formation, thioamide formation, reductive rearrangement, amination, hydrolysis.

pathways.name: Surface-only Cyanosulfidic Route from Hydroxyacetone
pathways.description: The parallel Patel et al. (2015) cyanosulfidic pathway starting from hydroxyacetone. Converges with P3 at the acetone hub.

pathways.name: Cyanosulfidic Pathway 2 via C6 Aminonitrile (from DHA)
pathways.description: Alternative cyanosulfidic route that produces valine from the C6 aminonitrile (2-amino-4-methylpentanenitrile) through hydrolysis with chain shortening. Upstream uses DHA route.

pathways.name: Cyanosulfidic Pathway 2 via C6 Aminonitrile (from Hydroxyacetone)
pathways.description: Alternative cyanosulfidic route via C6 aminonitrile using hydroxyacetone as the upstream sugar feedstock.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal Fischer-Tropsch to Surface Strecker Route
pathways.description: Isobutyraldehyde produced by hydrothermal Fischer-Tropsch-type synthesis on magnetite undergoes classical Strecker synthesis with HCN and NH3 in surface pools, converging at the aminonitrile hub before hydrolysis to valine.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal Reductive Amination Route
pathways.description: Direct hydrothermal route: alpha-ketoisovalerate synthesized on iron-sulfur minerals undergoes reductive amination with ammonia on pyrite/mackinawite surfaces to yield valine. Entirely hydrothermal, bypassing surface chemistry.

pathways.name: Full CO2-to-Valine Hydrothermal Reductive Amination
pathways.description: Complete hydrothermal pathway from CO2 through formate, formaldehyde, and alpha-ketoisovalerate to valine via reductive amination. Represents the deepest hydrothermal route.

pathways.name: Hybrid Hydrothermal-Surface Route: Formaldehyde to Strecker via Formose
pathways.description: Hydrothermal formaldehyde feeds formose chemistry producing glycolaldehyde and DHA in surface pools, with DHA entering the cyanosulfidic pathway. This traces the longest environmental interconnection chain.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Excellent primary pathway closely following Patel et al. (2015) cyanosulfidic protometabolism. The hydrothermal upstream (CO2 → formate → formaldehyde) is plausible though formate → formaldehyde on magnetite (rxn_002) is thermodynamically challenging. Formose steps (rxn_003, rxn_004) are well-known but should explicitly specify mineral catalysts (e.g., borate minerals). The core cyanosulfidic steps (rxn_006 through rxn_012) are directly supported by Patel et al. experimental evidence. Minor issue: rxn_006 should note Cu as a co-agent alongside H2S per literature. DHA → acetone stoichiometry should yield 2 H2O. Overall this is the strongest and most literature-supported pathway.
suggestions: Specify borate or clay mineral catalysts for formose reactions rxn_003 and rxn_004
Add Cu as explicit agent in rxn_006 and rxn_010 per Patel et al.
Consider thermodynamic barriers for formate → formaldehyde reduction (rxn_002)

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Parallel to P1 but routes through hydroxyacetone instead of DHA. rxn_005 (glycolaldehyde + formaldehyde → hydroxyacetone) is a less well-characterized formose variant compared to the DHA route in rxn_004; the mechanism for selective methylation needs clarification. Downstream cyanosulfidic steps are identical and well-supported. Provides valuable redundancy.
suggestions: Clarify mechanism for selective hydroxyacetone formation in rxn_005
Consider whether isomerization of glycolaldehyde/formaldehyde adduct is more realistic

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The most directly literature-supported pathway — this is essentially the Patel et al. (2015) cyanosulfidic route for valine synthesis starting from DHA. All six steps have experimental backing. Environmental progression score is lower because it operates entirely on the surface with no hydrothermal-surface transition, but this accurately reflects the literature. This is the highest-confidence chemical route.
suggestions: Explicitly note Cu/H2S agents in rxn_006 and rxn_010
Consider adding UV irradiation as environmental condition for surface cyanosulfidic pool

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Parallel surface-only route via hydroxyacetone. Nearly identical quality to P3. Both converge at acetone and follow the same downstream cyanosulfidic chemistry. Well-supported by Patel et al.
suggestions: Same Cu/H2S agent notation improvements as P3

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Critical gap: mol_017 (2-amino-4-methylpentanenitrile) has no production reaction in the network. No reaction node generates this C6 aminonitrile. Additionally, rxn_013 involves chain shortening from C6 to C5 during hydrolysis, which the literature acknowledges is poorly understood mechanistically. The pathway is incomplete as specified.
suggestions: Add explicit reaction producing mol_017 (2-amino-4-methylpentanenitrile) from upstream intermediates
Clarify the chain-shortening mechanism in rxn_013 — is it retro-aldol or decarboxylation?
Consider whether this pathway should be removed given the orphan intermediate

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Same critical issue as P5: mol_017 has no production reaction. The pathway is broken at the C6 aminonitrile node. Cannot be validated without the missing reaction.
suggestions: Same as P5 — add missing reaction for mol_017 production

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Alternative route via Fischer-Tropsch-type synthesis of isobutyraldehyde followed by classical Strecker synthesis. The FTT step (rxn_014) is plausible but selectivity for a specific branched C4 aldehyde is a significant concern — FTT typically produces complex product mixtures dominated by linear hydrocarbons. The Strecker step (rxn_015) is well-established. Provides good environmental flow from hydrothermal to surface. This represents a legitimate alternative prebiotic pathway but with lower confidence than the cyanosulfidic route.
suggestions: Specify mineral catalyst for FTT (e.g., iron-nickel alloys, awaruite)
Address selectivity concerns for branched aldehyde production
Consider adding intermediate steps between CO/H2 and isobutyraldehyde

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Minimal pathway requiring α-ketoisovalerate (mol_020) as input, with no clear upstream route specified in this pathway alone. The reductive amination (rxn_016) is chemically plausible under hydrothermal conditions but the availability of α-ketoisovalerate is the bottleneck. The pathway is too short and depends on an intermediate whose prebiotic synthesis is not well established.
suggestions: Must be evaluated in context of P9 which provides upstream chemistry
Specify mineral catalyst for hydrothermal reductive amination

pathway_evaluations.feedback: rxn_017 is the weakest reaction in the entire network. Converting formaldehyde + CO2 + H2 directly to α-ketoisovalerate (a C5 keto acid with a branched chain) is a massive chemical leap with no established prebiotic precedent. This effectively requires building a C5 branched carbon skeleton from C1 units in a single hydrothermal step without specifying intermediates. The description mentions 'from pyruvate and formaldehyde' but pyruvate itself is not a node in the network. This pathway has significant feasibility problems.
suggestions: Break rxn_017 into multiple steps: formaldehyde → pyruvate (e.g., via glycolaldehyde), then pyruvate + formaldehyde → α-ketoisovalerate
Add pyruvate as an intermediate node
Provide mineral catalyst specification for rxn_017
This pathway needs substantial reworking to be credible

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This appears to be essentially identical to P1, combining the full hydrothermal upstream with the surface cyanosulfidic downstream via the Strecker/formose hybrid. Same strengths and weaknesses as P1. Good environmental progression from hydrothermal C1 chemistry through surface formose to cyanosulfidic amino acid synthesis.
suggestions: Same improvements as P1

evaluation.network_feedback: This is a well-constructed synthesis network for L-valine that is strongly anchored in the Patel et al. (2015) cyanosulfidic protometabolism literature. The core strength lies in pathways P1-P4 and P10, which faithfully reproduce the experimentally demonstrated cyanosulfidic route with appropriate upstream hydrothermal feedstock chemistry. The five hub molecules create meaningful convergence points, and the network correctly identifies acetone as the critical branch-point intermediate. Key strengths: (1) Direct alignment with Patel et al. experimental work; (2) Multiple convergent routes providing redundancy; (3) Good hydrothermal-to-surface environmental flow; (4) Chemically sound core reactions. Key weaknesses: (1) mol_017 (2-amino-4-methylpentanenitrile) is an orphan node with no production reaction, breaking pathways P5 and P6; (2) rxn_017 (formaldehyde + CO2 + H2 → α-ketoisovalerate) is a single-step leap with no prebiotic precedent, undermining P9; (3) Surface formose reactions (rxn_003-005) should specify mineral catalysts; (4) Cu is not explicitly listed as an agent in the reaction descriptions for rxn_006, rxn_007, and rxn_010, despite being essential per the literature; (5) No biochemical environment stage is represented. The network would benefit from: adding the missing reaction producing mol_017, breaking rxn_017 into realistic sub-steps, and specifying all catalytic agents. Despite these issues, 5 of 10 pathways achieve sufficient confidence for approval, and the core chemistry is sound.
